Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## - WHAT TO PRUNE AND WHAT NOT TO PRUNE AT INI TIALIZATION

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Post-training dropout based approaches achieve high sparsity and are well established means of deciphering problems relating to computational cost and overfitting in Neural Network architectures citesrivastava2014dropout, (Pan et al., 2016), Zhu & Gupta (2017), LeCun et al. (1990). Contrastingly, pruning at initialization is still far behind Frankle et al. (2020). Initialization pruning is more efficacious when it comes to scaling computation cost of the network. Furthermore, it handles overfitting just as well as post training dropout. It is also averse to retraining losses.

In approbation of the above reasons, the paper presents two approaches to prune at initialization. The goal is to achieve higher sparsity while preserving performance. 1) K-starts, begins with k random p-sparse matrices at initialization. In the first couple of epochs the network then determines the ”fittest” of these p-sparse matrices in an attempt to find the ”lottery ticket” Frankle & Carbin (2018) p-sparse network. The approach is adopted from how evolutionary algorithms find the best individual. Depending on the Neural Network architecture, fitness criteria can be based on magnitude of network weights, magnitude of gradient accumulation over an epoch or a combination of both. 2) Dissipating gradients approach, aims at eliminating weights that remain within a fraction of their initial value during the first couple of epochs. Removing weights in this manner despite their magnitude best preserves performance of the network. Contrarily, the approach also takes the most epochs to achieve higher sparsity. 3) Combination of dissipating gradients and kstarts outperforms either methods and random dropout consistently.

The benefits of using the provided pertaining approaches are: 1) They do not require specific knowledge of the classification task, fixing of dropout threshold or regularization parameters 2) Retraining of the model is neither necessary nor affects the performance of the p-sparse network.
We evaluate the efficacy of the said methods on Autoencoders and Fully Connected Multilayered Perceptrons. The datasets used are MNIST and Fashion MNIST.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Computational complexity and overfitting in neural networks is a well established problem Frankle & Carbin (2018), Han et al. (2015), LeCun et al. (1990), Denil et al. (2013). We utilize pruning approaches for the following two reasons: 1) To reduce the computational cost of a fully connected neural network. 2) To reduce overfitting in the network.

Given a large number of post-training pruning approaches Srivastava et al. (2014), Geman et al. (1992), Pan et al. (2016), the paper attempts to propose two pre-training pruning approaches: kstarts and dissipating gradients. Moreover, it appears to be the case that when isolated from other factors sparse networks outperform fully connected networks. When not isolated they perform at least as well up to a percentage of sparsity depending on the number of parameters in the said network. kstarts and dissipating gradients provide are simple nevertheless effective methods to quickly look for best sparse networks to.
The approaches exploit the knowledge that a network has multiple underlying p-sparse networks that perform just as well and in some cases even better when contrasted with their fully connected

1

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

counterparts Frankle & Carbin (2018). What percentage of sparsity is realized, depends largely on the number of parameters originally present in the network. Such sparse networks are potent in preventing over-fitting and reducing computational cost.

The poset-training pruning has several approaches in place such as adding various regularization schemes to prune the network Louizos et al. (2017), Pan et al. (2016) or using second derivative or hessian of the weights for dropout LeCun et al. (1990), Hassibi & Stork (1993). Han et al. (2015), Alford et al. (2019), Zhu & Gupta (2017) use an efficient iterative pruning method to iteratively increase sparsity. Srivastava et al. (2014) dropout random hidden units with p probability instead of weights to avoid overfitting in general. Each of these approaches is effective and achieves good sparsity post-training.

We use a simple intuitive models that achieve good results and exploits the fact that a number of sub networks in a Neural Network has the potential to individually learn the input Srivastava et al. (2014). We decide on a sparse network early on based on the dropout method and use only that for training. This provides an edge for faster computation, quicker elimination of excess weights and reduced generalization error. The sparsity achieved is superior to random dropout.

Section II gives a general introduction to all the methods, section III defines p-sparsity, section IV provides the algorithm for both approaches, section V describes experimental setup and results, section VI discusses various design choices, section VII gives a general discussion of results, section VIII discusses limitations of the approach and section IX provides conclusions and final remarks.

## 2 PRUNING METHODS

## 2.1 KSTARTS

## 2.1.1 KSTARTS AND EVOLUTIONARY ALGORITHMS

We take the concept of k random starts from Evolutionary Algorithms (Vikhar, 2016) that use a fitness function or heuristic to perform ”natural selection” in optimization and search based problems (Goldberg & Holland, 1988). It is relatively simple to fit genetic algorithms to the problem at hand. Other method that would be equally effective with a little bit of modification are Hunting Search (Oftadeh et al., 2010), Natural Adaptation Strategies (Wierstra et al., 2008), firefly algorithm (Yang, 2010) etc.

The basic components of the algorithm are: (1) **Population** : A product of network weights and sparse matrices. (2) **Individual** : An instance of the population. (3) **Fitness Function** : The heuristic chosen for evaluation of the population.

## 2.1.2 POPULATION

We first initialize K sparse matrices, a single instance of these K sparse matrices can be seen in equation **??** . In every iteration we multiply model weights W of the Network layer in question with every instance of the K sparse matrices. The resulting set of matrices is our population for that iteration. Each iteration is referred to as a new generation.

_population_ = _W ∗ K − SparseMatrices_

(1)

## 2.1.3 INDIVIDUAL

Each individual, in a population of K instances, is a sparse matrix of size equal to the size of network weights, W. The number of 0’s and 1’s in the sparsity matrix are determined by the connectivity factor p which is further described in section 3. An sparse matrix of _p ≈_ 0 _._ 5 will have _≈_ 50% 0’s and _≈_ 50% 1s.

## 2.1.4 EVALUATION/FITNESS FUNCTION

The fitness of an individual is ranked by determining the sum of each individual in population as given in 1 such that the fittest individual in a generation is given by equation 2.

where _i ∗ c_ is the size of each individual and ind refers to the individual in population.

## 2.1.5 NEXT GENERATION SELECTION
Assuming each iteration is the next generation. In each generation the fit individual is favoured so:

- The fittest individual is passed of as weight to the next generation.

- Every 5 generations or as per the decided elimination frequency, the individual with lowest fitness is discarded from the population.

## 2.2 DISSIPATING GRADIENTS

Magnitude and gradient based pruning approaches are popular in post-training pruning Srivastava et al. (2014), LeCun et al. (1990). It does not make much sense to employ them pre-training becauseof the randomly generated weights. But in order to reduce error, any network aims to target updating weights that influence the results most. Based upon that hypothesis, in each epoch we sum gradients over and eliminate weights whose weights are not getting updated. In equation 3 N is the total number of iterations for an epoch. In equation 4 epsilon is 1e-6 for all experiments.

One consideration in this approach is to not do this for too many epochs which can be only 2 if the image is very monochrome and more than 2 if the gradients are dissipating more slowly. Moreover, once specific weights have reached their optimal learning, their gradients will dissipate and we don’t want to eliminate them.

## 2.3 COMBINATION DROPOUT

Combination dropout is merely combining Kstarts with dissipating gradients. The weights eliminated use both approaches. We fix p for Kstarts to a certain value of minimum sparsity and further eliminate weights that dissipating gradients method will eliminate as well. The approach achieves better performance than either methods.

## 3 DEFINING P-SPARSITY

In a p-sparse network layer approximately p percent of connection between two layers are eliminated. Figure 1 shows a fully connected conventional neural network (figure 1a) and three sparsely connected networks with different values of p (figures 1b, 1c, 1d).

## 3.1 CONNECTIVITY FACTOR

Connectivity factor, p, determines the percentage of connections to be removed between any two layers of the network. For instance if p = 0.0 than the network is be fully connected as shown in figure 1a, if on the opposite extreme, p=1.0, then there will be no connections between two layers of neurons. If p=0.5, figure 1b, only approximately 50% of the connection in the network remain. If p=0.3, figure 1c, approximately 70% of the connection still exist and if p=0.7, figure 1d, a mere 30% of the connections are active between two network layers. In short p determines percentage of 0’s in an individual sparse matrix shown in equation **??** .

## 4 ALGORITHM

The autoencoder, two and three layered neural networks are trained in a standard manner with adam optimizer and batch update.

## 4.1 KSTARTS ALGORITHM

Algorithm 1 gives the kstarts method:

1. We have K number of sparse matrices as shown in equation **??** generated and we call them KI in algorithm 1.

2. Every time the weight W needs to be updated instead of the usual gradient update we add one step further and using the fitness function, pass the fittest individual as the new W which biases the network towards that sparse matrix.

3. Every 5 iterations, the individual with lowest fitness is dropped.

**Algorithm 1:** k Random starts

**input :** Data, params,K,p **output:** W,b initialize W,b; _KI ←_ K individuals with approximately 1-p percent active connections; **for** _maxiterations_ **do** Run Neural Network; Update weights; **if** _One individual in KI is left_ **then** W _←_ individual; **else** W _←_ individual with maximum sum (of weights); **for** _every 5 iterations_ **do** pop individual with minimum sum (of weights) from KI; **end end end**

- 4.2 DISSIPATING GRADIENTS ALGORITHM

Algorithm 2 is more simple and just eliminates weights with sum of gradients equal to zero in the first 1-4 epochs depending on the desired sparsity.

Figure 2: Performance of dropout methods with increasing sparsity.

## 5 EXPERIMENTS AND RESULTS

The experiments performed on two datasets; MNIST Deng (2012) and Fashion MNIST Xiao et al. (2017). The network architectures are two layered Autoencoder (784-128-64), a three layered NN (784-128-100-10) both with sigmoid activation and adam optimization functions.

The Architecture used for learning curves is a single layered NN(784-10).

(a) Dataset : Fashion MNIST (b) Dataset : MNIST

Figure 3: Effect of sample size on learning when varying the sparsity of the network with kstarts. Each trial involves running a p-sparse network on a fixed training set of N examples from dataset for 2.5k iterations and is averaged over 10 trials each.

## 5.1 EFFECT OF INCREASING SPARSITY

As sparsity increases, overall performance reduces. Figure 2 shows the behaviour of various dropout methods presented in this paper.

In case of random dropout, it’s indeed a random shot. Either no useful weight is eliminated or multiple crucial weights are eliminated which decides how well does random dropout perform. Kstarts performs slightly better on average with multiple start choices. Depending on how many independent p-sparse networks are present in the network that can learn well, one of them can be identified, given k is large enough and the fitness function is smartly decided by first examining the weights and gradients.

Dissipating gradients works well as long as the network isn’t learning very fast i.e. some weights are being updated in the consequent epochs. It’s also most reliable. Combination works by far the best because it does not only rely on eliminating weights that are not being updated but also uses kstarts. It seems to achieve superior performance as long as p value chosen is a value that kstarts performs well on.

## 5.2 VARIATION IN SAMPLE SIZE

Figures 3a and 3b show relationship of varying sample size to different sparsity values in a single layer NN over 2.5k iterations.

The interesting result here is that isolated from all other factors like number of parameters, hidden units and various design choices, kstarts dropout performs better on a single layer network compared to even a fully connected network. The standard deviation is also lower to fully connected network as well as random dropout. kstarts dropout also learns faster than a fully connected network. For instance, if the iterations for the experiments in figure 3a and 3b are increased, the fully connected network will eventually reach accuracy of the p-sparse network.

## 6 DESIGN PARAMETER CHOICES

There are a number of design parameter choices for the algorithm 1 presented here. Some are explained in detail in this section.

## 6.1 CHOICE OF FITNESS FUNCTION

Since the fitness function determines the individual being passed on to the next generation and the individual being eliminated. We had three choices for choosing the fitness of an individual each with it’s own pros and cons.

6

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

- **Magnitude:** As opted here, we choose population as shown in equation 1 and then select fitness using equation 2. This skews the selection of new weights to the previously selected sparse matrix from KI and therefore, the initial sparse matrix will be propagated forward. This also renders elimination to be pointless it does not matter if 1 or all other matrices in K are eliminated. Furthermore, the sparse matrix is picked awfully early in the experiments i.e. only after first 5 iterations or so and that is not when weights have reached a saturation point.
- **Gradient:** The second choice is to use gradient of the weights to create population as follows:

- _population_ = _δW ∗ SparseMatrices_ (5)

- doing so can have fitness totally dependant on the current update and the new weights are heavily skewed towards the performance of the current iterations which again doesn’t seem that appropriate.
- **Sum of Gradients:** The third option is summing up the gradients for a number of iterations in the manner we do for dissipating gradients 3 and then use those to create population:

Doing so skews the network toward weights that are updated quickly and are increasing.

## 6.2 NO. OF LAYERS AND NO. HIDDEN UNITS

We initially use single layer NN (784-10) to isolate the effects of kstarts algorithm from effects of other parameters that may have a large impact on performance as the size of the network grows. Those parameters i.e. number of hidden layers, regularization choices, types of layers may aid or adversely effect the performance of the algorithm and by performing the experiments on the basic unit of a neural network we were able to concur that the method is effective in it’s own right. After concluding the approach works we tested on three layered NN and Autoencoder.

## 6.3 COST EFFECTIVESNESS

One beneficial feature of pre-training pruning approaches is that the best p-sparse network is quickly identified. There are a number of methods that exploit sparsity of a matrix for quicker multiplication i.e. (Yuster & Zwick, 2005), (Buluc¸ & Gilbert, 2012) which can be used to quickly retrain large networks. Although that is out of scope for our findings.

## 7 DISCUSSION

- 7.1 EFFECT OF K

1. From the experiments done so far lower K ( _≈_ 10) outperforms or at least performs as well as a higher value of K ( _≈_ 100). This can be because the more times a different matrix is chosen by the network, the more times the network has to adopt to learning with only that p-sparse network.

## 7.2 EFFECT OF P

1. A sparse network can outperforms a fully connected network for lower number of iterations and smaller networks.

2. An appropriate value of p, for instance in these experiments _p ≈_ 0 _._ 5, seems to work best for random dropout, kstarts dropout and combination dropout. A poor choice of p can’t seem to be remedied by a better choice of K.

3. p can be thought of as an information limiter. For better learning, if the network is only provided with particular features, it might have an easier time learning the class specific features in different nodes but this only remains a wishful speculation and requires further analysis. Table 1 shows relationship between k,p and no of iterations in a single layer NN (784-10)

## 8 LIMITATIONS

There are a number of limitations to the approach and further investigation is required in a number of domains.
1. The NNs used are single and three layered feed forward networks and autoencoders. CNNs are not experimented upon.
2. Only classification tasks are considered and those on only two datasets: MNIST and Fashion MNIST.
3. Ideally using sparse matrix should make for efficient computation but since the algorithms for that are not used it at this point does not show what the time comparison of the approaches will be.

## 9 CONCLUSIONS AND FINAL REMARKS

We present two methods for pruning weights pre-training or in the first couple of epochs. The comparisons are made against random dropout and both approaches mostly perform better than the random dropout. We provide a combination dropout approach that consistently outperforms other dropout approaches. A lot more analysis of the approach is required on multiple datasets, learning tasks and network architectures but the basic methods seem to be effective.

progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper presents a timely and conceptually interesting exploration of pruning neural networks at initialization, aiming to address computational efficiency and overfitting through two novel methods—kstarts and dissipating gradients—along with their combination. While the motivation is sound and the connection to the "lottery ticket hypothesis" (Frankle & Carbin, 2018) provides relevant context, the paper suffers from several critical issues that undermine its clarity, rigor, and overall contribution. Most notably, the writing is frequently unclear, grammatically flawed, and at times incoherent, which impedes comprehension of the proposed methods. Technical descriptions—especially of the kstarts algorithm—are vague and lack sufficient mathematical or algorithmic detail (e.g., undefined notation in Equation 2, unclear fitness computation, and ambiguous generational update mechanics), making reproducibility a serious concern. Furthermore, the dissipating gradients method is introduced incompletely—the text cuts off mid-sentence—and key intuitions, such as how gradients are monitored or why weight stability implies redundancy, are underdeveloped. The paper also lacks a clear comparison baseline, rigorous ablation studies, and evaluation on more complex architectures or datasets beyond MNIST and Fashion-MNIST, limiting the generalizability of the claims. While the idea of combining evolutionary-inspired selection with gradient-based pruning is promising, the absence of detailed experimental results, statistical validation, or comparison to state-of-the-art initialization-time pruning methods (e.g., SNIP, GraSP, or synaptic flow) significantly weakens the paper’s impact. Overall, the work shows potential but requires substantial revision in presentation, technical completeness, and empirical validation to meet the standards of a top-tier conference like ICLR.

comments.title: Misleading Description of Dissipating Gradients Criterion
comments.quote: dissipating gradients approach, aims at eliminating weights that remain within a fraction of their initial value during the first couple of epochs.
comments.explanation: This description is misleading. From algorithm 2 and section 2.2, the actual method eliminates weights whose accumulated gradient magnitude is below a threshold (effectively zero), not those whose weights remain close to initialization. These are distinct: a weight might change significantly even with small gradients over time, or stay near initial value despite large gradients (e.g., oscillation). The real criterion—'accumulated dW < 0.0001'—tracks gradient stagnation, not weight stagnation. While the section 2.2 title 'dissipating gradients' is accurate, the abstract's description incorrectly frames it in terms of weight values. This could mislead readers into thinking the method depends on weight trajectories, when it actually depends on gradient activity. This discrepancy between prose and mechanism is not resolved by surrounding context in the abstract, and constitutes a questionable claim.

comments.title: Incorrect logic in dissipating gradients algorithm: gradient accumulation threshold applied post-multiplication
comments.explanation: The algorithm in Figure 2 (Algorithm 2) accumulates gradients over inner iterations, applies a threshold (0.0001), then binarizes the accumulated gradient matrix: setting it to 0 if below threshold, else 1. Then it element-wise multiplies the current weights W by this mask. This means: W ← W ⊙ mask, where mask_{ij} = 1 if |accumulated dW_{ij}| >= 0.001, else 0. The concern is: this does **not** eliminate weights that 'remain close to their initial value' — it eliminates weights whose **gradients** were small. The method is called 'dissipating gradients', and the text says: 'eliminate weights whose weights are not getting updated' — but that's not what the algorithm does. It eliminates weights with small gradient *magnitude*, which is different. A weight could change a lot even with small gradients if updated many times (since dW is per-step). But the algorithm accumulates dW, so it's tracking total gradient flow. However, the actual weight change ΔW = sum(η * dW_t), so unless the learning rate is constant, accumulated dW is proportional to ΔW / η. So if η is fixed, accumulated |dW| correlates with |ΔW|. But the algorithm thresholds on accumulated dW, not on the actual weight change. The text in Section 2.2 says: 'eliminate weights whose weights are not getting updated' — which refers to small |ΔW|, not small |dW|. But the algorithm uses |sum dW| as a proxy. That might be reasonable, but it's not equivalent. More critically: after binarizing the gradient, it does W ← W * accumulated_dW_binary. This means: if a weight had large gradient, it remains; if not, it's zeroed out. But this sets W to zero, regardless of its current value. So even if a weight moved significantly, if its gradient sum was below threshold (e.g., due to noise cancellation), it gets pruned. Conversely, a weight with large gradient but little net change (oscillating) might survive. Worse: the algorithm runs for multiple epochs, and in each epoch, it accumulates dW, thresholds it, then masks W. But W itself is being updated in the loop — so pruning happens repeatedly. This means weights pruned in one epoch are zeroed, then in the next, their gradient will be zero (no contribution), so they stay zero. But more importantly, the pruning is applied to the current W, not the initial W. The method's stated idea is to eliminate weights that 'remain within a fraction of their initial value' — but the algorithm does not compare W to W_initial at all. It only uses gradient history. So there's a fundamental mismatch between the prose description and the algorithm. The title says 'dissipating gradients', and the mechanism is gradient-based, so perhaps the authors meant to prune based on gradient flow. But the text explicitly says 'eliminating weights that remain within a fraction of their initial value', which is a different criterion. This is a serious logical inconsistency. The algorithm implements a form of magnitude-of-gradient pruning over early training, but the justification given is based on lack of weight change. These are related but not equivalent. The claim that 'Removing weights in this manner despite their magnitude best preserves performance' is questionable because the method doesn't actually check magnitude or weight change. This undermines the interpretability and justification of the method.

comments.title: Missing threshold in dissipating gradients algorithm
comments.quote: if accumulated d W < 0 . 0001 then accumulated d W ← 0 ; else accumulated d W ← 1;
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 2 and Section 2.2, the threshold is given as 0.0001, but in the PAPER SUMMARY, it is stated as ε = 10⁻⁶. This is a direct contradiction. The summary notes: 'ε = 10⁻⁶' for the same condition. 0.0001 = 10⁻⁴, which is two orders of magnitude larger. This affects which weights are pruned: a higher threshold removes more weights, potentially harming performance. The authors claim consistency across experiments, but this discrepancy suggests either a typo in the algorithm or in the summary. Given that 10⁻⁶ is more typical for numerical precision in gradients, and 10⁻⁴ is unusually large, the algorithm listing likely contains an OCR or transcription error. However, without clarification, it's impossible to know which value was actually used. This undermines reproducibility and raises doubts about result validity.

comments.title: Unclear timing of pruning in dissipating gradients algorithm
comments.quote: W ← W*accumulated dW; end end
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 2, the weight masking step 'W ← W * accumulated dW' is placed *after* the epoch loop. But the text says pruning is based on gradient accumulation 'in the first 1-4 epochs'. If the pruning occurs only once, after all epochs, then accumulated dW has been summed over all epochs — which may include post-dissipation phases where gradients vanish not due to irrelevance but convergence. This contradicts the assumption that pruning should occur *before* gradient dissipation. The algorithm should reset or apply pruning at the end of early epochs (e.g., epoch 2), not after full training. Otherwise, it becomes a post-training method. The text claims 'eliminates weights ... in the first 1-4 epochs', but the algorithm does not reflect this. The code structure suggests pruning happens once, post-training, which invalidates the 'pruning at initialization' claim. This is a major flaw unless the 'maxepochs' is only 1–4, but the experiments run for 10k iterations, implying many epochs. Clarification is needed.

comments.title: Kstarts algorithm modifies W directly, potentially breaking backpropagation or weight updates
comments.quote: W _←_ individual with maximum sum (of weights);
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 1, the line 'W ← individual with maximum sum (of weights)' replaces the entire weight matrix W with a binary sparse matrix (the fittest individual) at every iteration. But this individual is a p-sparse mask (0s and 1s), not a weight matrix. Earlier definition says: 'Individual: An instance in the population, corresponding to one p-sparse matrix of same size as W'. And 'population = W * K-SparseMatrices', so individual is a sparse matrix of 0s and 1s. So replacing W with it sets all non-zero entries to 1, destroying the learned weight values. But then how are gradients computed? The next 'Update weights' step presumably applies gradient descent, but starting from a matrix of 0s and 1s. This would cause unstable training, as gradients are computed based on these artificial values. Moreover, the algorithm says 'every time the weight W needs to be updated ... we pass the fittest individual as the new W'. But that overwrites W before or after the gradient update? The loop is: Run NN → Update weights → then set W ← fittest individual. So after updating via gradients, they overwrite W with a binary mask. This completely discards the gradient update. That can't be right. Perhaps they mean to set W ← W * fittest_individual — i.e., apply the mask — not replace W with the mask. The population definition is W * K-SparseMatrices, suggesting that the sparse matrices are masks. So the fittest individual should be used as a mask: W ← W ⊙ mask, not W ← mask. Otherwise, all weight magnitudes are lost. This would be catastrophic for learning. The claim that 'the fittest subnetwork can be identified during early training' only makes sense if the subnetwork retains the learned weights. Replacing W with the mask sets all active weights to 1, which is not the 'lottery ticket' — the ticket is the combination of mask and initial weights. This indicates a fundamental flaw in the algorithm description. It's likely an OCR or LaTeX error — perhaps they meant W ← W * individual. In Section 2.1.3, 'individual' is defined as a sparse matrix of 0s and 1s, so replacing W with it is clearly wrong. There is no context that resolves this — the algorithm explicitly says 'W ← individual'. This would destroy the network at every step. The only way this makes sense is if 'individual' refers to W * sparse_matrix, not the sparse matrix itself. But the paper defines 'individual' as 'a sparse matrix', not the masked weights. This is a major issue in the core algorithm.

comments.title: Fitness function in kstarts lacks specification of which weights are summed
comments.quote: W _←_ individual with maximum sum (of weights);
comments.explanation: The algorithm says to select the individual with 'maximum sum (of weights)'. But the 'individual' is a binary sparse matrix (0s and 1s). Summing its entries would just give the number of 1s, which is approximately p * size, nearly constant across individuals. That provides no meaningful selection pressure. This only makes sense if 'sum of weights' refers to the sum of the *masked weights*, i.e., sum of W ⊙ individual, not sum of the individual itself. The population is defined as W * K-SparseMatrices — so each member is W ⊙ mask_k. Then evaluating fitness based on sum of magnitudes of that would make sense. But the algorithm text says 'individual with maximum sum (of weights)' — and individual is the mask. This is ambiguous. In Section 2.1.4: 'The fitness of an individual is ranked by determining the sum of each individual in population as given in 1' — and equation (1) is 'population = W * K-SparseMatrices'. So population is the set of masked W. Then 'sum of each individual in population' would mean sum(W ⊙ mask_k). That makes sense. But in Algorithm 1, they refer to 'individual' (the mask) when selecting based on sum of weights. This is a notation inconsistency: using 'individual' to mean both the sparse matrix and the population element. In context, 'individual' in the population should mean W ⊙ mask_k. But earlier, they define 'individual' as 'a sparse matrix'. This conflict is not resolved. Likely, it's a misuse of terminology. The algorithm should refer to selecting the population member with highest sum, then extract its mask for future use. But as written, it's unclear what is being summed. Given that Section 6.1 discusses using magnitude, gradient, or sum of gradients for fitness, it's clear they mean sum of |W| or |dW| over the subnetwork. So the intent is sound, but the algorithm mislabels the quantity being selected.

comments.title: Fitness Function Based on Weight Magnitude Misaligned with Assumptions
comments.quote: Magnitude: As opted here, we choose population as shown in equation 1 and then select fitness using equation 2. This skews the selection... early in the experiments i.e. only after first 5 iterations or so and that is not when weights have reached a saturation point.
comments.explanation: The authors explicitly state in the Key Claims that 'Using weight magnitude or instantaneous gradients for fitness selection can lead to premature or biased subnetwork selection', and they assume 'Optimal weights will have dissipated gradients after initial learning phase; pruning should occur before this dissipation'. Despite this sound reasoning, the Algorithm 1 uses 'maximum sum (of weights)' as the selection criterion — i.e., magnitude-based fitness — which directly contradicts their own critique. They acknowledge this flaw in Section 6.1, calling it problematic that selection happens 'awfully early' and renders elimination 'pointless'. Yet they present it as *the* algorithm without proposing or evaluating an alternative fitness function (e.g., based on accumulated gradients). This creates a tension: the method as implemented relies on a mechanism they themselves identify as flawed. While they explore gradient-based alternatives, they do not use them in results. This misalignment between critique and implementation weakens the credibility of the K-starts method as presented. The evaluation in Table 1 and Figure 3 may reflect performance of a suboptimal selection rule, not the potential of K-starts with better fitness design.

comments.title: Combination method underspecified: order and interaction of pruning steps undefined
comments.quote: We fix p for Kstarts to a certain value of minimum sparsity and further eliminate weights that dissipating gradients method will eliminate as well.
comments.explanation: The combination method is described as applying both kstarts and dissipating gradients, but the order, timing, and interaction are unclear. Does kstarts run first, selecting a subnetwork, then dissipating gradients prune within it? Or are both applied to the full network? The kstarts method involves a population of masks and evolution over iterations, while dissipating gradients accumulates gradients over epochs. How are these synchronized? Is the gradient accumulation done per individual or on the shared W? The algorithm suggests that W is being updated and masked globally, so dissipating gradients could apply to the final W, but kstarts implies multiple parallel masks. The text says 'the weights eliminated use both approaches' — but does that mean logical AND (prune if either method says prune)? Or sequential? The claim that combination 'achieves better performance' lacks sufficient detail for reproduction. Key parameters like when to apply each method, how p is chosen for kstarts in the combo, and whether the gradient accumulation is affected by the dynamic masking of kstarts are not specified. This is especially problematic because kstarts modifies W aggressively (as discussed), which would affect gradient values used in dissipating gradients. Without clarity on the integrated algorithm, the combination method cannot be reliably implemented or compared.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Fitness Function Definition and Missing Equation Reference
comments.quote:
The fitness of an individual is ranked by determining the sum of each individual in population as given in 1 such that the fittest individual in a generation is given by equation 2.

where _i ∗ c_ is the size of each individual and ind refers to the individual in population.
comments.explanation: At first, I assumed the fitness function was based on the sum of weights in the individual sparse matrix or the weighted sum after applying it to W. However, the reference to 'equation 1'—which defines the population as W ∗ K-SparseMatrices—and the mention of an undefined 'equation 2' raise concern. The text suggests that fitness is computed as the sum of each population member (i.e., W ∗ sparse_matrix), and the fittest individual corresponds to the one with maximum sum. But 'i ∗ c' is introduced without definition—possibly a misread OCR version of 'n' or 'm×n' for matrix dimensions? Even if so, no clear formula is provided for how the sum is computed (element-wise sum? L1 norm?). While the intention is likely to score each masked weight matrix by the magnitude of its retained weights, the absence of an actual equation (e.g., fitness(ind) = ||W ⊙ M_k||₁), and the broken reference (**??** earlier, now 'equation 2'), leaves the core mechanism underspecified. This is especially problematic because section 6.1 discusses using gradient or sum-of-gradients as alternatives, implying the default is based on weight magnitude—yet nowhere is this clearly formalized. Context partially resolves the intent (magnitude-based fitness), but the lack of a precise equation makes reproduction difficult.

comments.title: Inconsistent Definition of Connectivity Factor p
comments.quote: In a p-sparse network layer approximately p percent of connection between two layers are eliminated.
comments.explanation: At first glance, this seems consistent with the notation introduced in the PAPER SUMMARY, where p ≈ 0.5 implies 50% zeros. However, upon closer inspection, the sentence says 'p percent of connection... are eliminated', which would imply that for p = 0.5, 0.5% of connections are removed — an interpretation that is wildly at odds with the rest of the paper. This is especially problematic because later in Section 3.1, the text correctly states that 'if p=0.5, ... only approximately 50% of the connection in the network remain'. This suggests that p should represent the *fraction* of connections removed, not a percentage in the numerical sense. The confusion arises because 'p percent' is used literally (implying division by 100), but p is a decimal between 0 and 1. For example, if p = 0.5, saying 'p percent' would mean 0.5%, not 50%. This is a serious ambiguity: a careful reader could misinterpret p as being on a 0–100 scale rather than 0–1. Although context (e.g., figure descriptions and equation usage) eventually resolves this, the phrasing is dangerously misleading in a foundational definition. The authors correctly use p ∈ [0,1] throughout experiments and tables (e.g., p=0.5), so the *intent* is clear, but the prose introduces an avoidable confusion between proportion and percentage.

comments.title: Inaccurate comparison of random dropout: confuses weight vs. unit dropout
comments.quote: Contrastingly, pruning at initialization is still far behind Frankle et al. (2020). ... Srivastava et al. (2014) dropout random hidden units with p probability instead of weights to avoid overfitting
comments.explanation: The paper criticizes initialization pruning as 'far behind' existing methods, citing Frankle et al. (2020), but then compares its methods to 'random dropout', which it describes as randomly dropping hidden units (citing Srivastava et al., 2014). This is accurate — standard dropout does drop units. However, when the paper says 'random dropout' in results (e.g., Figure 2), it seems to use it as a baseline pruning method that removes weights, not units. But unit dropout is not equivalent to weight sparsity — it's a regularization technique during training, not a structural pruning. The experimental comparison of 'random dropout' likely means random *weight* pruning at initialization, which is not what Srivastava et al. do. The text conflates two different concepts: dropout as a training technique and dropout as a pruning method. This undermines the fairness of comparison. Moreover, Frankle et al. (2020) show that many initialization pruning methods underperform lottery ticket rewinding, but the paper here claims to outperform 'random dropout' — which is a low bar. The more relevant comparison would be to other initialization pruning methods like SNIP, GraSP, or edge-popup. By comparing only to random dropout, the claimed advantage may be misleading. The method might indeed be better than random pruning, but asserting it 'outperforms random dropout' without clarifying that this is not the standard use of dropout is questionable.

comments.title: Incorrect gradient-based population definition using δW instead of dW
comments.quote: _population_ = _δW ∗ SparseMatrices_ (5)
comments.explanation: In Section 6.1, the authors propose an alternative fitness function using gradients, defining the population as δW ∗ SparseMatrices. The use of δW is problematic because this symbol is nonstandard — typically, ∂W or dW denotes gradients. The PAPER SUMMARY and Algorithm 2 use dW for gradient, which is standard. This may be an OCR error (δ vs d), but δ is a Greek delta, whereas d is Latin — and they appear distinct here. If intentional, δW could imply a variation or update, but the more serious issue is conceptual: defining the population as gradient-masked matrices (rather than weight-masked) changes the meaning of 'population' from Section 2.1.3, which is defined as W ∗ SparseMatrices. If population suddenly becomes gradient-based, then the evolutionary analogy breaks: individuals are no longer sparse subnetworks but transient gradient patterns. This would fundamentally misalign the method. The authors likely meant to use accumulated dW (as in dissipating gradients) to score individuals, not to redefine the population. The current formulation suggests that weights are replaced by gradients, which is incorrect. This is a serious conceptual flaw unless interpreted as a proposal for fitness scoring only — but even then, it's ambiguously phrased. The text says 'use gradient of the weights to create population', implying a structural change, not just evaluation.

comments.title: Table 1 column labeling inconsistency
comments.explanation: The table header lists p=0.0 and then two columns both labeled p=0.5, but the subcolumns are labeled k=1, k=10, k=50, k=100. This suggests that p=0.0 corresponds only to k=1, and p=0.5 spans k=10 to k=100. But that contradicts the experimental design: k is the number of initial sparse matrices, independent of p. More likely, the first p=0.5 should apply to k=1 and k=10, and the second p=0.5 to k=50 and k=100 — but that would mean p is fixed and k varies, which makes sense. The problem is that the column for k=1 appears under p=0.0, but k=10 under p=0.5 — with no k entries under p=0.0 except k=1. This implies k=1 was tested at both p=0.0 and p=0.5? But p=0.0 is fully connected, so k should be irrelevant (only one possible mask). Yet accuracy for k=1, p=0.0 at 10k iterations is 82.49, while for k=1 at p=0.5 it's 88.40 — suggesting two different runs. The labeling is ambiguous: it likely means the left block is p=0.0 (with k irrelevant, so only k=1 shown), and the right block is p=0.5 with varying k. The double p=0.5 is a formatting error — one should be omitted. This could mislead a reader into thinking p varied within the same condition. The data itself seems fine, but the presentation risks misinterpretation.

progressive_original__qwen3-235b-a22b-25.label: Progressive Original (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
progressive_original__qwen3-235b-a22b-25.overall_feedback: The paper presents a timely and conceptually interesting exploration of pruning neural networks at initialization, aiming to address computational efficiency and overfitting through two novel methods—kstarts and dissipating gradients—along with their combination. While the motivation is sound and the connection to the "lottery ticket hypothesis" (Frankle & Carbin, 2018) provides relevant context, the paper suffers from several critical issues that undermine its clarity, rigor, and overall contribution. Most notably, the writing is frequently unclear, grammatically flawed, and at times incoherent, which impedes comprehension of the proposed methods. Technical descriptions—especially of the kstarts algorithm—are vague and lack sufficient mathematical or algorithmic detail (e.g., undefined notation in Equation 2, unclear fitness computation, and ambiguous generational update mechanics), making reproducibility a serious concern. Furthermore, the dissipating gradients method is introduced incompletely—the text cuts off mid-sentence—and key intuitions, such as how gradients are monitored or why weight stability implies redundancy, are underdeveloped. The paper also lacks a clear comparison baseline, rigorous ablation studies, and evaluation on more complex architectures or datasets beyond MNIST and Fashion-MNIST, limiting the generalizability of the claims. While the idea of combining evolutionary-inspired selection with gradient-based pruning is promising, the absence of detailed experimental results, statistical validation, or comparison to state-of-the-art initialization-time pruning methods (e.g., SNIP, GraSP, or synaptic flow) significantly weakens the paper’s impact. Overall, the work shows potential but requires substantial revision in presentation, technical completeness, and empirical validation to meet the standards of a top-tier conference like ICLR.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Fitness Function Definition and Missing Equation Reference
comments.quote:
The fitness of an individual is ranked by determining the sum of each individual in population as given in 1 such that the fittest individual in a generation is given by equation 2.

where _i ∗ c_ is the size of each individual and ind refers to the individual in population.
comments.explanation: At first, I assumed the fitness function was based on the sum of weights in the individual sparse matrix or the weighted sum after applying it to W. However, the reference to 'equation 1'—which defines the population as W ∗ K-SparseMatrices—and the mention of an undefined 'equation 2' raise concern. The text suggests that fitness is computed as the sum of each population member (i.e., W ∗ sparse_matrix), and the fittest individual corresponds to the one with maximum sum. But 'i ∗ c' is introduced without definition—possibly a misread OCR version of 'n' or 'm×n' for matrix dimensions? Even if so, no clear formula is provided for how the sum is computed (element-wise sum? L1 norm?). While the intention is likely to score each masked weight matrix by the magnitude of its retained weights, the absence of an actual equation (e.g., fitness(ind) = ||W ⊙ M_k||₁), and the broken reference (**??** earlier, now 'equation 2'), leaves the core mechanism underspecified. This is especially problematic because section 6.1 discusses using gradient or sum-of-gradients as alternatives, implying the default is based on weight magnitude—yet nowhere is this clearly formalized. Context partially resolves the intent (magnitude-based fitness), but the lack of a precise equation makes reproduction difficult.

comments.title: Mismatch Between Stated Number of Methods and Presentation
comments.quote: the paper presents two approaches to prune at initialization. ... 1) K-starts ... 2) Dissipating gradients approach ... 3) Combination of dissipating gradients and kstarts
comments.explanation: The abstract opens by stating the paper presents 'two approaches,' but then clearly lists three: kstarts, dissipating gradients, and their combination. This initially seemed contradictory. However, the third is explicitly described as a combination, so the framing as 'two core approaches' is justified. The issue is minor because the structure of the paper (sections 2.1, 2.2, 2.3) confirms this interpretation. Still, a more precise phrasing like 'two core methods and their combination' in the abstract would prevent confusion. Given this is standard academic shorthand and the rest of the paper supports it, the issue is resolved by context.

comments.title: Misleading Description of Dissipating Gradients Criterion
comments.quote: dissipating gradients approach, aims at eliminating weights that remain within a fraction of their initial value during the first couple of epochs.
comments.explanation: This description is misleading. From algorithm 2 and section 2.2, the actual method eliminates weights whose accumulated gradient magnitude is below a threshold (effectively zero), not those whose weights remain close to initialization. These are distinct: a weight might change significantly even with small gradients over time, or stay near initial value despite large gradients (e.g., oscillation). The real criterion—'accumulated dW < 0.0001'—tracks gradient stagnation, not weight stagnation. While the section 2.2 title 'dissipating gradients' is accurate, the abstract's description incorrectly frames it in terms of weight values. This could mislead readers into thinking the method depends on weight trajectories, when it actually depends on gradient activity. This discrepancy between prose and mechanism is not resolved by surrounding context in the abstract, and constitutes a questionable claim.

comments.title: OCR Artifact in Equation 1 Suggests Operation Ambiguity
comments.quote: _population_ = _W ∗ K − SparseMatrices_
comments.explanation: The expression 'W ∗ K − SparseMatrices' is syntactically invalid. 'K − SparseMatrices' suggests subtraction of the number K from a matrix, which makes no sense. This is almost certainly an OCR misreading of 'W ∗ K_SparseMatrices' or 'W ∗ {M_k}', where '{M_k}' is a set or list of K sparse matrices. Given the surrounding description—'multiply model weights W ... with every instance of the K sparse matrices'—the intended operation is element-wise multiplication (Hadamard product) of W with each of the K sparse masks, resulting in K masked weight matrices forming the population. This interpretation is supported by the evolutionary analogy (each individual is a variation of W). While OCR errors like this are expected, and the meaning is recoverable from context, the symbol confusion between 'K' (scalar count) and a representation of the set of matrices is a notable notation inconsistency that could mislead a reader scanning quickly.

comments.title: Missing Formal Definition of Key Algorithm Component
comments.quote: The fittest individual is passed of as weight to the next generation.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'passed of' is clearly a typo for 'passed on'. The bigger issue is the mechanism: replacing W with the sparse matrix? That can't be right—sparse masks are 0/1; W has real values. What is likely intended is that the fittest sparse matrix (mask) is selected, and future updates are applied only within its support—i.e., W is updated normally but then masked by the selected M_k. Or perhaps only the masked weights (W ⊙ M_k) are used forward. But algorithm 1 later says 'W ← individual', where individual is a sparse matrix of 0s and 1s, which would destroy all weight values. This contradicts the goal of preserving performance. Section 6.1 also notes that using magnitude-based fitness leads to early propagation of a mask, but doesn't clarify if W is being replaced or masked. This is a critical underspecification: if W is literally replaced with the binary mask, the network loses all learned weight magnitudes. More plausibly, the mask is applied (W := W ⊙ M) and training continues. But this must be explicitly stated. The method is underspecified at a key point.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of 'p' in p-sparsity: inconsistent with percentage of connections eliminated
comments.quote:
In a p-sparse network layer approximately p percent of connection between two layers are eliminated.

Connectivity factor, p, determines the percentage of connections to be removed between any two layers of the network. For instance if p = 0.0 than the network is be fully connected as shown in figure 1a, if on the opposite extreme, p=1.0, then there will be no connections between two layers of neurons. If p=0.5, figure 1b, only approximately 50% of the connection in the network remain.
comments.explanation: At first glance, this seems consistent: if p is the fraction of connections removed, then p=0.5 means 50% are removed → 50% remain. But the initial PAPER SUMMARY states: 'a sparse matrix with p ≈ 0.5 has approximately 50% zeros and 50% ones' — which matches. However, the confusion arises when p=0.3 is described as 'approximately 70% of the connection still exist' — that implies only 30% removed, i.e., p=0.3 means 30% removed. This is consistent with the definition. Yet later, p=0.7 says 'a mere 30% of the connections are active' — which again implies 70% are removed, so p=0.7 means 70% removed. So far, so good. However, the notation becomes ambiguous in practice. In Table 1, p=0.0 corresponds to fully connected (784×10 weights), and p=0.5 shows improved performance. But if p is the *fraction of connections removed*, then p=0.5 means 50% sparsity. However, in standard literature (e.g., Lottery Ticket Hypothesis), sparsity is often defined as the fraction of weights remaining, or the fraction pruned. Here, the authors define p as the fraction *eliminated*, which is acceptable — but their prose in Section 3.1 says: 'p determines percentage of 0’s in an individual sparse matrix'. That would imply p is the fraction of zeros, i.e., the sparsity level — which aligns with convention. But then p=0.5 means 50% zeros, which matches. However, when they say 'p ≈ 0.5' has '≈ 50% 0’s and ≈ 50% 1s', it is consistent. The real issue is in the sentence: 'if p=0.3, figure 1c, approximately 70% of the connection still exist' — that means 30% are removed, so p=0.3 → 30% removed, 70% active. But that conflicts with the idea that p is *defined* as the fraction of zeros — unless the matrix has 30% zeros. But that would be consistent. The problem is not in the math, but in the wording: they interchangeably use 'p percent of connection ... eliminated' and 'p determines percentage of 0’s' — which are equivalent *only if* we assume the connection mask is binary and p is the zero fraction. That is in fact what they do. So the definition is internally consistent. However, the phrase 'p percent of connection ... eliminated' is dangerously ambiguous: 'p percent' could be misread as (p × 100)% — e.g., p=0.5 → 0.5% — which is absurd. But given the context of p=0.5, p=1.0, and figures, that misreading is avoided. Still, a more precise phrasing like 'a fraction p of the connections are eliminated' would avoid confusion. Given that the authors consistently use p ∈ [0,1] and describe it as the fraction (not percentage) of zeros or removed connections, and the summary confirms 'p ≈ 0.5 has 50% zeros', the definition is correct but the phrasing 'p percent' is misleading. This is a minor linguistic ambiguity that could cause misreading by a non-expert.

comments.title: Incorrect logic in dissipating gradients algorithm: gradient accumulation threshold applied post-multiplication
comments.explanation: The algorithm in Figure 2 (Algorithm 2) accumulates gradients over inner iterations, applies a threshold (0.0001), then binarizes the accumulated gradient matrix: setting it to 0 if below threshold, else 1. Then it element-wise multiplies the current weights W by this mask. This means: W ← W ⊙ mask, where mask_{ij} = 1 if |accumulated dW_{ij}| >= 0.001, else 0. The concern is: this does **not** eliminate weights that 'remain close to their initial value' — it eliminates weights whose **gradients** were small. The method is called 'dissipating gradients', and the text says: 'eliminate weights whose weights are not getting updated' — but that's not what the algorithm does. It eliminates weights with small gradient *magnitude*, which is different. A weight could change a lot even with small gradients if updated many times (since dW is per-step). But the algorithm accumulates dW, so it's tracking total gradient flow. However, the actual weight change ΔW = sum(η * dW_t), so unless the learning rate is constant, accumulated dW is proportional to ΔW / η. So if η is fixed, accumulated |dW| correlates with |ΔW|. But the algorithm thresholds on accumulated dW, not on the actual weight change. The text in Section 2.2 says: 'eliminate weights whose weights are not getting updated' — which refers to small |ΔW|, not small |dW|. But the algorithm uses |sum dW| as a proxy. That might be reasonable, but it's not equivalent. More critically: after binarizing the gradient, it does W ← W * accumulated_dW_binary. This means: if a weight had large gradient, it remains; if not, it's zeroed out. But this sets W to zero, regardless of its current value. So even if a weight moved significantly, if its gradient sum was below threshold (e.g., due to noise cancellation), it gets pruned. Conversely, a weight with large gradient but little net change (oscillating) might survive. Worse: the algorithm runs for multiple epochs, and in each epoch, it accumulates dW, thresholds it, then masks W. But W itself is being updated in the loop — so pruning happens repeatedly. This means weights pruned in one epoch are zeroed, then in the next, their gradient will be zero (no contribution), so they stay zero. But more importantly, the pruning is applied to the current W, not the initial W. The method's stated idea is to eliminate weights that 'remain within a fraction of their initial value' — but the algorithm does not compare W to W_initial at all. It only uses gradient history. So there's a fundamental mismatch between the prose description and the algorithm. The title says 'dissipating gradients', and the mechanism is gradient-based, so perhaps the authors meant to prune based on gradient flow. But the text explicitly says 'eliminating weights that remain within a fraction of their initial value', which is a different criterion. This is a serious logical inconsistency. The algorithm implements a form of magnitude-of-gradient pruning over early training, but the justification given is based on lack of weight change. These are related but not equivalent. The claim that 'Removing weights in this manner despite their magnitude best preserves performance' is questionable because the method doesn't actually check magnitude or weight change. This undermines the interpretability and justification of the method.

comments.title: Kstarts algorithm modifies W directly, potentially breaking backpropagation or weight updates
comments.quote: W _←_ individual with maximum sum (of weights);
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 1, the line 'W ← individual with maximum sum (of weights)' replaces the entire weight matrix W with a binary sparse matrix (the fittest individual) at every iteration. But this individual is a p-sparse mask (0s and 1s), not a weight matrix. Earlier definition says: 'Individual: An instance in the population, corresponding to one p-sparse matrix of same size as W'. And 'population = W * K-SparseMatrices', so individual is a sparse matrix of 0s and 1s. So replacing W with it sets all non-zero entries to 1, destroying the learned weight values. But then how are gradients computed? The next 'Update weights' step presumably applies gradient descent, but starting from a matrix of 0s and 1s. This would cause unstable training, as gradients are computed based on these artificial values. Moreover, the algorithm says 'every time the weight W needs to be updated ... we pass the fittest individual as the new W'. But that overwrites W before or after the gradient update? The loop is: Run NN → Update weights → then set W ← fittest individual. So after updating via gradients, they overwrite W with a binary mask. This completely discards the gradient update. That can't be right. Perhaps they mean to set W ← W * fittest_individual — i.e., apply the mask — not replace W with the mask. The population definition is W * K-SparseMatrices, suggesting that the sparse matrices are masks. So the fittest individual should be used as a mask: W ← W ⊙ mask, not W ← mask. Otherwise, all weight magnitudes are lost. This would be catastrophic for learning. The claim that 'the fittest subnetwork can be identified during early training' only makes sense if the subnetwork retains the learned weights. Replacing W with the mask sets all active weights to 1, which is not the 'lottery ticket' — the ticket is the combination of mask and initial weights. This indicates a fundamental flaw in the algorithm description. It's likely an OCR or LaTeX error — perhaps they meant W ← W * individual. In Section 2.1.3, 'individual' is defined as a sparse matrix of 0s and 1s, so replacing W with it is clearly wrong. There is no context that resolves this — the algorithm explicitly says 'W ← individual'. This would destroy the network at every step. The only way this makes sense is if 'individual' refers to W * sparse_matrix, not the sparse matrix itself. But the paper defines 'individual' as 'a sparse matrix', not the masked weights. This is a major issue in the core algorithm.

comments.title: Fitness function in kstarts lacks specification of which weights are summed
comments.quote: W _←_ individual with maximum sum (of weights);
comments.explanation: The algorithm says to select the individual with 'maximum sum (of weights)'. But the 'individual' is a binary sparse matrix (0s and 1s). Summing its entries would just give the number of 1s, which is approximately p * size, nearly constant across individuals. That provides no meaningful selection pressure. This only makes sense if 'sum of weights' refers to the sum of the *masked weights*, i.e., sum of W ⊙ individual, not sum of the individual itself. The population is defined as W * K-SparseMatrices — so each member is W ⊙ mask_k. Then evaluating fitness based on sum of magnitudes of that would make sense. But the algorithm text says 'individual with maximum sum (of weights)' — and individual is the mask. This is ambiguous. In Section 2.1.4: 'The fitness of an individual is ranked by determining the sum of each individual in population as given in 1' — and equation (1) is 'population = W * K-SparseMatrices'. So population is the set of masked W. Then 'sum of each individual in population' would mean sum(W ⊙ mask_k). That makes sense. But in Algorithm 1, they refer to 'individual' (the mask) when selecting based on sum of weights. This is a notation inconsistency: using 'individual' to mean both the sparse matrix and the population element. In context, 'individual' in the population should mean W ⊙ mask_k. But earlier, they define 'individual' as 'a sparse matrix'. This conflict is not resolved. Likely, it's a misuse of terminology. The algorithm should refer to selecting the population member with highest sum, then extract its mask for future use. But as written, it's unclear what is being summed. Given that Section 6.1 discusses using magnitude, gradient, or sum of gradients for fitness, it's clear they mean sum of |W| or |dW| over the subnetwork. So the intent is sound, but the algorithm mislabels the quantity being selected.

comments.title: Combination method underspecified: order and interaction of pruning steps undefined
comments.quote: We fix p for Kstarts to a certain value of minimum sparsity and further eliminate weights that dissipating gradients method will eliminate as well.
comments.explanation: The combination method is described as applying both kstarts and dissipating gradients, but the order, timing, and interaction are unclear. Does kstarts run first, selecting a subnetwork, then dissipating gradients prune within it? Or are both applied to the full network? The kstarts method involves a population of masks and evolution over iterations, while dissipating gradients accumulates gradients over epochs. How are these synchronized? Is the gradient accumulation done per individual or on the shared W? The algorithm suggests that W is being updated and masked globally, so dissipating gradients could apply to the final W, but kstarts implies multiple parallel masks. The text says 'the weights eliminated use both approaches' — but does that mean logical AND (prune if either method says prune)? Or sequential? The claim that combination 'achieves better performance' lacks sufficient detail for reproduction. Key parameters like when to apply each method, how p is chosen for kstarts in the combo, and whether the gradient accumulation is affected by the dynamic masking of kstarts are not specified. This is especially problematic because kstarts modifies W aggressively (as discussed), which would affect gradient values used in dissipating gradients. Without clarity on the integrated algorithm, the combination method cannot be reliably implemented or compared.

comments.title: Inaccurate comparison of random dropout: confuses weight vs. unit dropout
comments.quote: Contrastingly, pruning at initialization is still far behind Frankle et al. (2020). ... Srivastava et al. (2014) dropout random hidden units with p probability instead of weights to avoid overfitting
comments.explanation: The paper criticizes initialization pruning as 'far behind' existing methods, citing Frankle et al. (2020), but then compares its methods to 'random dropout', which it describes as randomly dropping hidden units (citing Srivastava et al., 2014). This is accurate — standard dropout does drop units. However, when the paper says 'random dropout' in results (e.g., Figure 2), it seems to use it as a baseline pruning method that removes weights, not units. But unit dropout is not equivalent to weight sparsity — it's a regularization technique during training, not a structural pruning. The experimental comparison of 'random dropout' likely means random *weight* pruning at initialization, which is not what Srivastava et al. do. The text conflates two different concepts: dropout as a training technique and dropout as a pruning method. This undermines the fairness of comparison. Moreover, Frankle et al. (2020) show that many initialization pruning methods underperform lottery ticket rewinding, but the paper here claims to outperform 'random dropout' — which is a low bar. The more relevant comparison would be to other initialization pruning methods like SNIP, GraSP, or edge-popup. By comparing only to random dropout, the claimed advantage may be misleading. The method might indeed be better than random pruning, but asserting it 'outperforms random dropout' without clarifying that this is not the standard use of dropout is questionable.

comments.title: Inconsistent definition of p in connectivity factor description
comments.quote: In a p-sparse network layer approximately p percent of connection between two layers are eliminated.
comments.explanation: Initially, this statement appears to conflict with the definition in the PAPER SUMMARY, which says that p ≈ 0.5 corresponds to 50% zeros and 50% ones. However, the quote implies that p is the proportion of connections *eliminated*, which would mean p = 0.5 → 50% removed → 50% remaining, aligning with the summary. The confusion arises in Section 3.1, where it says: 'if p = 0.0 then the network is fully connected' and 'if p = 1.0, then there will be no connections'. This supports the definition that p is the fraction of connections *removed*, not retained. However, later in the same section, it says: 'if p = 0.5, figure 1b, only approximately 50% of the connection in the network remain' — which is consistent. The real problem is in the sentence: 'p determines percentage of 0’s in an individual sparse matrix'. This directly equates p with the fraction of zeros, which *is* consistent. So, the definition is actually consistent throughout. The concern resolves upon closer reading: p is indeed the proportion of zeros (i.e., eliminated connections), and the text does not contradict itself. What initially seemed like an error was a misparse of 'p percent of connection are eliminated' — which actually correctly means p is the fraction eliminated. No real inconsistency remains.

comments.title: Ambiguity in fitness function evaluation via 'sum of each individual in population'
comments.quote: The fitness of an individual is ranked by determining the sum of each individual in population as given in 1 such that the fittest individual in a generation is given by equation 2.
comments.explanation: The text refers to 'sum of each individual in population as given in 1', but Equation (1) defines the population as W ∗ K-SparseMatrices, which is a set of masked weight matrices. It's unclear what 'sum' refers to: sum of absolute weights? sum of squared weights? sum of gradient magnitudes? Later, in Section 6.1, the authors clarify that for the 'Magnitude' fitness function, they use the sum of weights (or perhaps magnitude) in the masked matrix. However, the original description (Section 2.1.4) fails to specify this, and no Equation (2) is visible in the provided text. This makes the method underspecified at first. While the later discussion in Section 6.1 provides some clarification, the core algorithm description lacks a formal definition of how fitness is computed. A reader would not be able to reproduce the method from the initial algorithm description alone. This is a gap in method specification that undermines reproducibility, even though intent becomes clearer later.

comments.title: Incorrect gradient-based population definition using δW instead of dW
comments.quote: _population_ = _δW ∗ SparseMatrices_ (5)
comments.explanation: In Section 6.1, the authors propose an alternative fitness function using gradients, defining the population as δW ∗ SparseMatrices. The use of δW is problematic because this symbol is nonstandard — typically, ∂W or dW denotes gradients. The PAPER SUMMARY and Algorithm 2 use dW for gradient, which is standard. This may be an OCR error (δ vs d), but δ is a Greek delta, whereas d is Latin — and they appear distinct here. If intentional, δW could imply a variation or update, but the more serious issue is conceptual: defining the population as gradient-masked matrices (rather than weight-masked) changes the meaning of 'population' from Section 2.1.3, which is defined as W ∗ SparseMatrices. If population suddenly becomes gradient-based, then the evolutionary analogy breaks: individuals are no longer sparse subnetworks but transient gradient patterns. This would fundamentally misalign the method. The authors likely meant to use accumulated dW (as in dissipating gradients) to score individuals, not to redefine the population. The current formulation suggests that weights are replaced by gradients, which is incorrect. This is a serious conceptual flaw unless interpreted as a proposal for fitness scoring only — but even then, it's ambiguously phrased. The text says 'use gradient of the weights to create population', implying a structural change, not just evaluation.

comments.title: Logical inconsistency in K-starts elimination mechanism vs. algorithm behavior
comments.quote: This skews the selection of new weights to the previously selected sparse matrix from KI and therefore, the initial sparse matrix will be propagated forward. This also renders elimination to be pointless it does not matter if 1 or all other matrices in K are eliminated.
comments.explanation: In Section 6.1, the authors correctly observe that selecting the fittest individual (by magnitude) early causes that sparse mask to be retained and propagated, making elimination of other individuals irrelevant — because once one dominates, the others are never used. This insight is valid and critical. However, Algorithm 1 says: 'W ← individual with maximum sum (of weights)' — which implies W is updated to the *values* of the masked weights, not just the mask. But that would overwrite the learned weights with an initial random sparse matrix, which is catastrophic. The intended behavior is likely that the *mask* of the fittest individual is used to prune W, not that W is replaced by the masked values. The text says 'pass the fittest individual as the new W', but this must mean 'use its sparsity pattern to mask W'. Otherwise, learning is reset every time. This misstatement suggests a fundamental flaw, but context in the evolutionary analogy and pruning goal suggests the authors mean mask selection. Still, the algorithm as written would destroy learned weights. This is a major risk of misinterpretation and erroneous implementation.

comments.title: Table 1 column labeling inconsistency
comments.explanation: The table header lists p=0.0 and then two columns both labeled p=0.5, but the subcolumns are labeled k=1, k=10, k=50, k=100. This suggests that p=0.0 corresponds only to k=1, and p=0.5 spans k=10 to k=100. But that contradicts the experimental design: k is the number of initial sparse matrices, independent of p. More likely, the first p=0.5 should apply to k=1 and k=10, and the second p=0.5 to k=50 and k=100 — but that would mean p is fixed and k varies, which makes sense. The problem is that the column for k=1 appears under p=0.0, but k=10 under p=0.5 — with no k entries under p=0.0 except k=1. This implies k=1 was tested at both p=0.0 and p=0.5? But p=0.0 is fully connected, so k should be irrelevant (only one possible mask). Yet accuracy for k=1, p=0.0 at 10k iterations is 82.49, while for k=1 at p=0.5 it's 88.40 — suggesting two different runs. The labeling is ambiguous: it likely means the left block is p=0.0 (with k irrelevant, so only k=1 shown), and the right block is p=0.5 with varying k. The double p=0.5 is a formatting error — one should be omitted. This could mislead a reader into thinking p varied within the same condition. The data itself seems fine, but the presentation risks misinterpretation.

comments.title: Missing threshold in dissipating gradients algorithm
comments.quote: if accumulated d W < 0 . 0001 then accumulated d W ← 0 ; else accumulated d W ← 1;
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 2 and Section 2.2, the threshold is given as 0.0001, but in the PAPER SUMMARY, it is stated as ε = 10⁻⁶. This is a direct contradiction. The summary notes: 'ε = 10⁻⁶' for the same condition. 0.0001 = 10⁻⁴, which is two orders of magnitude larger. This affects which weights are pruned: a higher threshold removes more weights, potentially harming performance. The authors claim consistency across experiments, but this discrepancy suggests either a typo in the algorithm or in the summary. Given that 10⁻⁶ is more typical for numerical precision in gradients, and 10⁻⁴ is unusually large, the algorithm listing likely contains an OCR or transcription error. However, without clarification, it's impossible to know which value was actually used. This undermines reproducibility and raises doubts about result validity.

comments.title: Unclear timing of pruning in dissipating gradients algorithm
comments.quote: W ← W*accumulated dW; end end
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 2, the weight masking step 'W ← W * accumulated dW' is placed *after* the epoch loop. But the text says pruning is based on gradient accumulation 'in the first 1-4 epochs'. If the pruning occurs only once, after all epochs, then accumulated dW has been summed over all epochs — which may include post-dissipation phases where gradients vanish not due to irrelevance but convergence. This contradicts the assumption that pruning should occur *before* gradient dissipation. The algorithm should reset or apply pruning at the end of early epochs (e.g., epoch 2), not after full training. Otherwise, it becomes a post-training method. The text claims 'eliminates weights ... in the first 1-4 epochs', but the algorithm does not reflect this. The code structure suggests pruning happens once, post-training, which invalidates the 'pruning at initialization' claim. This is a major flaw unless the 'maxepochs' is only 1–4, but the experiments run for 10k iterations, implying many epochs. Clarification is needed.

comments.title: Inconsistent Definition of Connectivity Factor p
comments.quote: In a p-sparse network layer approximately p percent of connection between two layers are eliminated.
comments.explanation: At first glance, this seems consistent with the notation introduced in the PAPER SUMMARY, where p ≈ 0.5 implies 50% zeros. However, upon closer inspection, the sentence says 'p percent of connection... are eliminated', which would imply that for p = 0.5, 0.5% of connections are removed — an interpretation that is wildly at odds with the rest of the paper. This is especially problematic because later in Section 3.1, the text correctly states that 'if p=0.5, ... only approximately 50% of the connection in the network remain'. This suggests that p should represent the *fraction* of connections removed, not a percentage in the numerical sense. The confusion arises because 'p percent' is used literally (implying division by 100), but p is a decimal between 0 and 1. For example, if p = 0.5, saying 'p percent' would mean 0.5%, not 50%. This is a serious ambiguity: a careful reader could misinterpret p as being on a 0–100 scale rather than 0–1. Although context (e.g., figure descriptions and equation usage) eventually resolves this, the phrasing is dangerously misleading in a foundational definition. The authors correctly use p ∈ [0,1] throughout experiments and tables (e.g., p=0.5), so the *intent* is clear, but the prose introduces an avoidable confusion between proportion and percentage.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Dissipating Gradients Algorithm Implementation
comments.quote: if accumulated d W < 0 . 0001 then accumulated d W ← 0 ; else accumulated d W ← 1;
comments.explanation: This line in Algorithm 2 uses a threshold of 0.0001, but in the PAPER SUMMARY, the threshold ε is defined as 10⁻⁶ (i.e., 0.000001) for the same condition. This discrepancy raises concern: is this a typo, an OCR error, or a real inconsistency? The abstract and Section 2.2 both refer to 'a fraction of their initial value' and 'epsilon is 1e-6', which aligns with the summary. However, here it's 1e-4. Given that gradient magnitudes can vary significantly, especially in early training, the choice of threshold matters: 1e-4 may retain fewer weights than 1e-6, leading to higher sparsity but potentially pruning important ones. While OCR might have corrupted the number (e.g., '1e-6' misread as '0.0001' due to '1e-6' looking like '10 6' and reinterpreted), the value 0.0001 is numerically 1e-4, not 1e-6. There's no explanation in the text for this difference. The rest of the paper assumes consistency in ε, and Table 1 suggests good performance, so perhaps the experiments used 1e-4. But without clarification, a reader cannot reproduce the method exactly. This constitutes an underspecification: the threshold is a critical hyperparameter, and its inconsistent reporting undermines reproducibility.

comments.title: Kstarts Algorithm Lacks Selection Frequency Specification
comments.explanation: While Algorithm 1 specifies that the worst individual is removed every 5 iterations, the paper does not clarify what happens if multiple low-fitness individuals exist or if this process continues until only one remains. The condition 'if One individual in KI is left' suggests the algorithm continues until |KI| = 1, but with K=100 and elimination every 5 iterations, this would take 495 iterations — which may exceed the early pruning window (1–4 epochs). Moreover, in Table 1, results are reported for as few as 10 iterations, yet for K=100, no eliminations would have occurred by then (first drop at iteration 5, then 10, etc.), so multiple individuals remain. The algorithm states 'W ← individual with maximum sum (of weights)' in that case, but it's unclear whether this is per iteration or only after selection. A more fundamental issue is that the algorithm does not specify how the 'fittest' individual is used to update W *during* propagation — is W entirely replaced by the binary mask times original weights?
